one_line_takeaway: Poison-RAG shows that stealthily editing only item tags in a text-based RAG recommender can reliably demote popular items via a local, semantic-relevance-weighted tag-selection attack, but promoting long-tail items remains hard and naive global edits backfire by boosting the very items they target.
key_finding: Demoting popular items is feasible but promoting long-tail items is hard, and the local strategy clearly beats the global one. Local tag modifications reduce popularity lift (e.g., under the Decay profile in the cold-start/original setting, baseline PLift 0.9380 drops to 0.9191 at size 1 and 0.9282 at size 3, ~1-2% reductions; under Rating, baseline 0.9314 drops to as low as 0.8754), and consistently lower popular-item relevance (popular HR@1 falls ~5-6%, e.g., from 0.3229 to 0.3021 before reranking and 0.2708 to 0.2552 after). Global modifications frequently backfire, inflating popularity lift to 1.3938-1.8537 and raising popular-item HR@1 from 0.3229 to 0.4010-0.4375 (a 24-35% increase) -- an attack failure that ironically makes the attack less detectable. Long-tail items resist promotion (long-tail HR@1 generally drops, e.g., 0.0909 -> 0.0303). Effects are consistent across retrieval and post-reranking stages. The abstract summarizes local strategies as improving manipulation effectiveness by up to 50%. Data augmentation offers only partial defense, mainly stabilizing/boosting mid-tail items (e.g., mid-tail HR@1 0.3483 -> 0.3652) which were not explicitly targeted.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Poison-RAG, a provider-side (producer-side) data-poisoning attack framework against retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)-based recommender systems. The attacker perturbs only free-text item metadata (tags), leaving title and genre untouched for stealth, in order to shift item popularity classes: promoting long-tail (unpopular) items and demoting popular ones in the recommendation output. Its own novel piece is an adversarial tag-selection method that scores a candidate tag t for item i as A'(t,i) = A(t) * s(t,i), combining an 'adversarial impact' term A(t) = log(P(t|c_target)/(P(t|c_orig)+epsilon)) (log-ratio of tag frequency in the target vs. original popularity class) with a 'semantic relevance' term s(t,i) = cosine similarity between BERT embeddings of the tag and item, so modifications stay contextually plausible/imperceptible. Two strategies are proposed: local (a personalized candidate tag pool per item, drawn from semantically similar items in the opposite popularity class) and global (a single uniform tag pool for the whole dataset). A secondary contribution is a proposed defense direction: LLM-based data augmentation (GPT-3.5-turbo enriched descriptions + auto-tagging) to address the cold-start of items with no tags and to add partial resilience.
threat_model: Black-box, provider/producer-side attacker. Knowledge: no access to internal model parameters or architecture; can only observe the system's output recommendations. Capability (power): can modify only item tags in the metadata (not title, genre, or user interaction data); a bounded number of tags k per item is altered (experiments use k = 1, 3, 5). Objective: maximize cumulative exposure of long-tail items while minimizing exposure of popular items across all users, formalized as an objective over Exposure(i,u) defined either as a binary indicator of appearing in R'(u) or as continuous 1/log2(rank_u(i)+1). Attack success is measured by shifts in popularity class and ranking position. Stealth is a design goal: keeping genre/title fixed and choosing semantically relevant tags is intended to keep manipulations inconspicuous to users.
limitations: Single dataset (MovieLens ml-latest-small) and a small, aggressively filtered corpus (365 users, 3079 items, 16,823 interactions, 1.50% density), limiting generalizability. The tested RAG pipeline is itself LLM-synthesized: descriptions and tags are produced by GPT-3.5-turbo/NLP rather than being a deployed production system, so the attack surface is partly constructed by the authors. The stated goal (promoting long-tail items) largely fails, so the framework mainly demonstrates demotion, not balanced manipulation. ~70% of items originally lack tags (cold-start), which both motivates and confounds the setup. The proposed data-augmentation defense is only partially evaluated and its benefits to mid-tail items are described as incidental. The paper is largely a short empirical study; no adaptive defense is implemented, and there is no comparison against external attack baselines from prior RAG-poisoning work (BadRAG, PoisonedRAG) -- those are only cited as related work, not run.

datasets_used: MovieLens 'ml-latest-small' (ratings, item info, user-generated tags), filtered to users with 20-100 interactions; final stats: |U|=365, |I|=3079, |R|=16,823, avg 46.09 interactions/user, 5.46/item, density 1.50%
quantitative_results: Popularity Lift (Decay, original/cold-start): baseline 0.9380 -> Local 0.9191 (size 1) / 0.9282 (size 3) (~2% and ~1% reduction, success); Global rises to 1.3938 / 1.6235 (failure)
Popularity Lift (Rating, original): baseline 0.9314 -> Local as low as 0.8754 (size 5); Global up to 1.8537
Popularity Lift (Decay, with data augmentation): baseline 1.3600 -> Local 0.9304 (size 1) / 0.9516 (size 3); Global 1.1994 / 1.1614
Popular-item HR@1 (data-augmented): 0.3229 -> 0.3021 before reranking, 0.2708 -> 0.2552 after reranking (~5-6% drop, aligns with attacker goal)
Global strategy popular-item HR@1: rises 0.3229 -> 0.4010 / 0.4375 (24-35% increase, attack failure)
Mid-tail HR@1 (data augmentation, incidental defense benefit): 0.3483 -> 0.3652
Mean Popularity Rank (Decay, original): baseline 26.961 -> Local 26.418 (size 1); Global up to 46.67
Long-Tail Coverage stays very low across settings (e.g., Decay baseline 0.0073 -> Local ~0.0045), showing long-tail items are hard to surface
Local strategy improves manipulation effectiveness by up to ~50% (abstract summary)
Context stats: ~60% of LLM applications integrate RAG; ~70% of items lack tags (cold-start)
User-profile temporal decay: lambda=0.01, power factor alpha=1.2; tag-modification sizes tested k=1,3,5
baselines_compared: Local attack strategy vs Global attack strategy
Original / cold-start setting vs Data Augmentation setting
Before reranking vs after LLM reranking (cutoff = 10)
Rating-weighted vs temporal Decay-weighted user profiles
